### Law

Main article: [Law of Germany](/wiki/Law_of_Germany "Law of Germany")

Further information: [Judiciary of Germany](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Germany "Judiciary of Germany") and [Law enforcement in Germany](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Germany "Law enforcement in Germany")

Germany has a [civil law system](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") based on [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law") with some references to [Germanic law](/wiki/Germanic_law "Germanic law").[[144]](#cite_note-149) The *[Bundesverfassungsgericht](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court_of_Germany "Federal Constitutional Court of Germany")* (Federal Constitutional Court) is the German Supreme Court responsible for constitutional matters, with power of [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review").[[145]](#cite_note-150) Germany's specialised supreme court system includes the [Federal Court of Justice](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Justice "Federal Court of Justice") for civil and criminal cases, along with the [Federal Labour Court](/wiki/Federal_Labour_Court "Federal Labour Court"), [Federal Social Court](/wiki/Federal_Social_Court "Federal Social Court"), [Federal Fiscal Court](/wiki/Federal_Fiscal_Court "Federal Fiscal Court"), and [Federal Administrative Court](/wiki/Federal_Administrative_Court_(Germany) "Federal Administrative Court (Germany)") for other matters.[[146]](#cite_note-151)

Criminal and private laws are codified on the national level in the *[Strafgesetzbuch](/wiki/Strafgesetzbuch "Strafgesetzbuch")* and the *[Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")* respectively. The German penal system seeks the [rehabilitation of the criminal](/wiki/Rehabilitation_(penology) "Rehabilitation (penology)") and the protection of the public.[[147]](#cite_note-152) With the exceptions of petty crimes, tried by a single professional judge, and of serious political crimes, all charges are adjudicated by mixed tribunals where [lay judges](/wiki/Lay_judge "Lay judge") (*[Schöffen](/wiki/Sch%C3%B6ffe "Schöffe")*) and professional judges preside together.[[148]](#cite_note-153)[[149]](#cite_note-154)

In 2016, Germany's murder rate stood at a low of 1.18 murders per 100,000.[[150]](#cite_note-155) In 2018, the overall [crime rate in the country](/wiki/Crime_in_Germany "Crime in Germany") fell to its lowest since 1992,[[151]](#cite_note-156) while in 2024, it was reported that violent crime reached a 15-year high, with overall crime rising as well.[[152]](#cite_note-157)

[Same-sex marriage in Germany](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Germany "Same-sex marriage in Germany") has been legal since 2017, and [LGBT rights in the country](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Germany "LGBT rights in Germany") are generally protected.[[153]](#cite_note-158)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Germany](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Germany "Foreign relations of Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Group_photo_of_the_G7_members_at_the_Scholss_Elmau_summit_%282%29.jpg/250px-Group_photo_of_the_G7_members_at_the_Scholss_Elmau_summit_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Group_photo_of_the_G7_members_at_the_Scholss_Elmau_summit_(2).jpg)

Germany hosted the [2022 G7 summit](/wiki/2022_G7_summit "2022 G7 summit") at [Schloss Elmau](/wiki/Schloss_Elmau "Schloss Elmau") in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria").

Germany has a network of [227 diplomatic missions abroad](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_Germany "List of diplomatic missions in Germany")[[154]](#cite_note-159) and maintains relations with more than 190 countries.[[155]](#cite_note-160) Germany is a member of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"), the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and the [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"). It has played an influential role in the European Union since its inception[[156]](#cite_note-161) and has maintained [a strong alliance with France](/wiki/Franco-German_cooperation "Franco-German cooperation")[[157]](#cite_note-162) and all neighbouring countries since 1990. Germany promotes the creation of a [more unified European](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") political, economic and security apparatus.[[158]](#cite_note-163) Because of its [economic power](/wiki/Economic_power "Economic power") and political influence, Germany is widely considered to be a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power").[[159]](#cite_note-Milena_Steriob-164)[[160]](#cite_note-PaulWirtz2004-165)

The governments of [Germany and the United States](/wiki/Germany%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Germany–United States relations") are close political allies.[[161]](#cite_note-166) Cultural ties and economic interests have crafted a bond between the two countries resulting in [Atlanticism](/wiki/Atlanticism "Atlanticism").[[162]](#cite_note-167)

Germany's development policy functions as a distinct sector within its foreign policy framework. It is formulated by the Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development and carried out by the implementing organisations. The German government sees development policy as a joint responsibility of the international community.[[163]](#cite_note-168) Germany was the world's [second-biggest aid donor](/wiki/List_of_development_aid_sovereign_state_donors "List of development aid sovereign state donors") in 2019 after the United States.[[164]](#cite_note-169)

### Military

Main article: [Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Leopard_2_A5_der_Bundeswehr.jpg/250px-Leopard_2_A5_der_Bundeswehr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leopard_2_A5_der_Bundeswehr.jpg)

[Leopard 2](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2") battle tanks of the German Army

Germany's military, the *Bundeswehr* (Federal Defence), is organised into the *[Heer](/wiki/German_Army "German Army")* (Army and special forces [KSK](/wiki/Kommando_Spezialkr%C3%A4fte "Kommando Spezialkräfte")), *[Marine](/wiki/German_Navy "German Navy")* (Navy), *[Luftwaffe](/wiki/German_Air_Force "German Air Force")* (Air Force) and *[Cyber- und Informationsraum](/wiki/Cyber_and_Information_Domain_Service "Cyber and Information Domain Service")* (Cyber and Information Domain Service) branches.[[165]](#cite_note-170) In absolute terms, German military spending in 2024 was the [fourth-highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures").[[166]](#cite_note-SIPRI-2020-171) In response to the 2022 [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War"), Chancellor Olaf Scholz announced that German military expenditure would be increased past the NATO target of 2%, along with a one-time 2022 infusion of 100 billion euros, representing almost double the 53 billion euro military budget for 2021.[[167]](#cite_note-172)[[168]](#cite_note-173) In 2023, military spending according to NATO criteria amounted to $73.1 billion, or 1.64% of the country's GDP, well below the NATO target of 2%. In 2024, Germany reported $97.7 billion to NATO, exceeding said target at 2.12% of GDP.[[169]](#cite_note-:0-174)

As of May 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit), the *Bundeswehr* has a strength of 180,215 active soldiers and 80,761 civilians.[[170]](#cite_note-175) Reservists are available to the armed forces and participate in defence exercises and deployments abroad.[[171]](#cite_note-bwzukunft-176) Until 2011, [military service was compulsory](/wiki/Conscription_in_Germany "Conscription in Germany") for men at age 18, but this has been officially suspended and replaced with a voluntary service.[[172]](#cite_note-177)[[173]](#cite_note-178) Since 2001 women may serve in all functions of service without restriction.[[174]](#cite_note-179) According to the [Stockholm International Peace Research Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_International_Peace_Research_Institute "Stockholm International Peace Research Institute"), Germany was the [fifth-largest exporter of major arms in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_arms_exports "List of countries by arms exports") between 2020 and 2024.[[175]](#cite_note-180)

In peacetime, the *Bundeswehr* is commanded by the [Minister of Defence](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(Germany) "Minister of Defence (Germany)"). In a [state of defence](/wiki/State_of_defence "State of defence"), the Chancellor would become commander-in-chief of the *Bundeswehr*.[[176]](#cite_note-181) The role of the *Bundeswehr* is described in the [Constitution of Germany](/wiki/Constitution_of_Germany "Constitution of Germany") as defensive only. But after a ruling of the Federal Constitutional Court in 1994, the term "defence" has been defined not only to include protection of the borders of Germany, but also crisis reaction and conflict prevention, or more broadly as guarding the [security](/wiki/Security "Security") of Germany anywhere in the world. As of 2017,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit) the German military has about 3,600 troops stationed in foreign countries as part of international peacekeeping forces, including about 1,200 supporting operations against [Daesh](/wiki/Daesh "Daesh"), 980 in the NATO-led [Resolute Support Mission](/wiki/Resolute_Support_Mission "Resolute Support Mission") in Afghanistan, and 800 in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo").[[177]](#cite_note-182)[[178]](#cite_note-183)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Germany](/wiki/Economy_of_Germany "Economy of Germany")

See also: [Science and technology in Germany](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Germany "Science and technology in Germany") and [List of German inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_German_inventions_and_discoveries "List of German inventions and discoveries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Frankfurt_Skyline_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Skyline_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Skyline_2022_(cropped).jpg)

[Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), a leading business and financial centre in Europe and the seat of the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank")

Germany has a [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy") with a highly skilled [labour force](/wiki/Labour_force "Labour force"), a low level of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Germany "Corruption in Germany"),[[179]](#cite_note-184) and a high level of [innovation](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation").[[180]](#cite_note-185) It is the [largest economy in Europe by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of sovereign states in Europe by GDP (nominal)"), as well as the world's [third-largest by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)")[[181]](#cite_note-186) and [sixth-largest by PPP-adjusted GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)").[[182]](#cite_note-187) Its PPP-adjusted GDP per capita amounted to 115% of the EU average in 2024.[[183]](#cite_note-188) The [country's service sector](/wiki/Service_sector_in_Germany "Service sector in Germany") contributes approximately 72% of the total GDP, the industrial sector 27%—with Germany having the [largest manufacturing output in Europe](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output")—and [its agricultural sector](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Germany "Agriculture in Germany") 1%, as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit).[[115]](#cite_note-CIA-120) [The unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_German_states_by_unemployment_rate "List of German states by unemployment rate") published by [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") amounts to 3.2% as of January 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit), which is the [fourth-lowest in the EU](/wiki/List_of_European_Union_member_states_by_unemployment_rate "List of European Union member states by unemployment rate").[[184]](#cite_note-Eurostat-189)

Germany is part of the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market") which represents more than 450 million consumers.[[185]](#cite_note-190) In 2017, the country accounted for 28% of the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") economy according to the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").[[186]](#cite_note-imf.org-191) Germany introduced the common EU currency, the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), in 2002.[[187]](#cite_note-euroc-192) Its monetary policy is set by the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"),[[188]](#cite_note-193) which is based in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), the country's largest financial centre.[[189]](#cite_note-frankfurt-194) Germany is the world's [third-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and [third-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports"),[[115]](#cite_note-CIA-120) and it has the [second-largest trade surplus](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_net_goods_exports "List of countries by net goods exports") after China. [Its largest trading partners](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Germany "List of the largest trading partners of Germany") in 2025 were China, the United States and the Netherlands.[[190]](#cite_note-195) [Germany's main exports](/wiki/List_of_exports_of_Germany "List of exports of Germany") are vehicles, machinery, and chemical goods.[[191]](#cite_note-Destatis2-196)

The [German automotive industry](/wiki/German_automotive_industry "German automotive industry") is among the most competitive and innovative in the world.[[192]](#cite_note-197) It was the [sixth-largest by production](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production") and largest by export value in 2023.[[193]](#cite_note-198) Germany is home to [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group"), the world's [second-largest automotive manufacturer by vehicle production](/wiki/List_of_automotive_manufacturers_by_production "List of automotive manufacturers by production").[[194]](#cite_note-199)

Of the world's 500 largest stock market-listed companies by revenue in 2024, the [*Fortune* Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500"), 29 were based in Germany.[[195]](#cite_note-200) The [DAX](/wiki/DAX "DAX"), Germany's stock market index operated by the [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange"), includes 30 major Germany-based companies.[[196]](#cite_note-201) Prominent [German companies](/wiki/German_companies "German companies") include [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz"), [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW"), [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"), [Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi"), [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche"), [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"), [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), [SAP](/wiki/SAP "SAP"), [Bosch](/wiki/Robert_Bosch_GmbH "Robert Bosch GmbH") and [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF"). [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") is a [hub](/wiki/Startup_ecosystem "Startup ecosystem") for [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") and has become the leading location for venture capital-funded firms in the EU.[[197]](#cite_note-202) Germany is recognised for its large portion of specialised [small and medium enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium_enterprises "Small and medium enterprises") known as the *[Mittelstand](/wiki/Mittelstand "Mittelstand")*;[[198]](#cite_note-203) these companies represent around 48% of the global market leaders in their segments, labelled [hidden champions](/wiki/Hidden_champions "Hidden champions").[[199]](#cite_note-204)

[Research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") efforts form an integral part of the German economy,[[200]](#cite_note-205) with the country [ranking fourth](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_research_and_development_spending "List of sovereign states by research and development spending") in research and development expenditure since 2005.[[201]](#cite_note-206) In 2018, Germany [ranked fourth](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_scientific_and_technical_journal_articles "List of countries by number of scientific and technical journal articles") globally in terms of number of science and engineering research papers published[[202]](#cite_note-207) and third in the quality-adjusted [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index") in 2023.[[203]](#cite_note-208) Well-known research institutions in Germany include the [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society"), the [Helmholtz Association](/wiki/Helmholtz_Association_of_German_Research_Centres "Helmholtz Association of German Research Centres"), the [Fraunhofer Society](/wiki/Fraunhofer_Society "Fraunhofer Society"), and the [Leibniz Association](/wiki/Leibniz_Association "Leibniz Association").[[204]](#cite_note-209) Germany is the largest contributor to the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency").[[205]](#cite_note-210) The country was ranked 11th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[206]](#cite_note-211)

### Infrastructure

Main articles: [Transport in Germany](/wiki/Transport_in_Germany "Transport in Germany"), [Energy in Germany](/wiki/Energy_in_Germany "Energy in Germany"), [Telecommunications in Germany](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Germany "Telecommunications in Germany"), and [Water supply and sanitation in Germany](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Germany "Water supply and sanitation in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/ICE_3_Oberhaider-Wald-Tunnel.jpg/250px-ICE_3_Oberhaider-Wald-Tunnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:ICE_3_Oberhaider-Wald-Tunnel.jpg)

High-speed trains like this [ICE 3](/wiki/ICE_3 "ICE 3") connect the [whole of Germany](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Germany "High-speed rail in Germany").

With its central position in Europe, Germany is a transport hub for the continent.[[207]](#cite_note-212) Its road network is among the [densest in Europe](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_road_network_density "List of countries by road network density").[[208]](#cite_note-213) The motorway (*[Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn")*) is widely known for having no general federally mandated [speed limit](/wiki/Speed_limits_in_Germany "Speed limits in Germany") for some classes of vehicles.[[209]](#cite_note-wa_16-09-2013-214) The [Intercity Express](/wiki/Intercity_Express "Intercity Express") or *ICE* train network serves major German cities as well as destinations in neighbouring countries with speeds up to 300 km/h (190 mph).[[210]](#cite_note-215) The largest [German airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Germany "List of airports in Germany") are [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport"), [Munich Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport") and [Berlin Brandenburg Airport](/wiki/Berlin_Brandenburg_Airport "Berlin Brandenburg Airport").[[211]](#cite_note-216) The [Port of Hamburg](/wiki/Port_of_Hamburg "Port of Hamburg") is the [third-busiest port in Europe](/wiki/List_of_busiest_ports_in_Europe "List of busiest ports in Europe") and one of the twenty [largest container ports in the world](/wiki/List_of_world%27s_busiest_container_ports "List of world's busiest container ports").[[212]](#cite_note-217)

In 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit), Germany was the world's [seventh-largest consumer of energy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_energy_consumption "List of countries by energy consumption").[[213]](#cite_note-218) All [German nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plants_in_Germany "Nuclear power plants in Germany") were phased out in 2023.[[214]](#cite_note-219) Germany meets its power demands using 40% [renewable sources](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") (2018),[[215]](#cite_note-220) and has been called an "early leader" in [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panel "Solar panel") and [offshore wind](/wiki/Offshore_wind_power "Offshore wind power").[[216]](#cite_note-International_Energy_Agency-2021-221) The [German energy transition](/wiki/Energy_transition_in_Germany "Energy transition in Germany") (*Energiewende*) is the recognised move to a sustainable economy by means of energy efficiency and renewable energy,[[217]](#cite_note-222)[[218]](#cite_note-International_Energy_Agency-20212-223) with the country being called "the world's first major renewable energy economy".[[219]](#cite_note-224) Germany has reduced its [primary energy consumption](/wiki/Primary_energy_consumption "Primary energy consumption") by 11% between 1990 and 2015[[220]](#cite_note-225) and set itself goals of reducing it by 30% until 2030 and 50% by 2050.[[221]](#cite_note-226)

The country is committed to the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") and several other treaties promoting biodiversity,[[222]](#cite_note-227) low emission standards,[[223]](#cite_note-228) and [water management](/wiki/Water_management "Water management").[[224]](#cite_note-229) As of 2017, Germany's household recycling rate is among the [highest in the world](/wiki/Recycling_rates_by_country "Recycling rates by country"), at around 65%.[[225]](#cite_note-230) In 2023, Germany was the 14th highest emitting nation of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas").[[226]](#cite_note-231)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Germany](/wiki/Tourism_in_Germany "Tourism in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Brandenburger_Tor_abends.jpg/250px-Brandenburger_Tor_abends.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brandenburger_Tor_abends.jpg)

The [Brandenburg Gate](/wiki/Brandenburg_Gate "Brandenburg Gate") in Berlin

Domestic and international travel and tourism directly contributed over €105.3 billion to German GDP in 2015.[[227]](#cite_note-:1-232) Including indirect and induced impacts, the industry supported nearly 4.2 million jobs in 2015.[[227]](#cite_note-:1-232) As of 2024, Germany is the [seventh-most-visited country](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings").[[228]](#cite_note-233) Its most popular landmarks include [Cologne Cathedral](/wiki/Cologne_Cathedral "Cologne Cathedral"), the [Brandenburg Gate](/wiki/Brandenburg_Gate "Brandenburg Gate"), the [Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building"), the [Dresden Frauenkirche](/wiki/Dresden_Frauenkirche "Dresden Frauenkirche"), [Neuschwanstein Castle](/wiki/Neuschwanstein_Castle "Neuschwanstein Castle"), [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), the [Wartburg](/wiki/Wartburg "Wartburg"), and [Sanssouci Palace](/wiki/Sanssouci_Palace "Sanssouci Palace").[[229]](#cite_note-234) [Europa-Park](/wiki/Europa-Park "Europa-Park") near [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg_im_Breisgau "Freiburg im Breisgau") is Europe's second-most popular theme park resort.[[230]](#cite_note-235)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Germany](/wiki/Demographics_of_Germany "Demographics of Germany")

See also: [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans")

With a population of 84.7 million according to the 2023 German census,[[231]](#cite_note-236) Germany is the most populous [member state of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"), the [second-most populous country in Europe](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_population "List of European countries by population")[[f]](#cite_note-turkey-238) after [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"),[[115]](#cite_note-CIA-120) and the [nineteenth-most populous country in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"). Its [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") stands at 236 inhabitants per square kilometre (610 inhabitants/mi2). The [fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of 1.57 children born per woman (2022 estimates) is below the replacement rate of 2.1 and is one of the [lowest in the world](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_fertility_rate "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by fertility rate").[[115]](#cite_note-CIA-120) Since the 1970s, Germany's [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") has exceeded its [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate"). However, the country is witnessing increased birth and migration rates since the early 2010s. Germany has the [fourth oldest population in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age"), with an average age of 47.8 years.[[115]](#cite_note-CIA-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Hubschrauberflug_Hamburg.jpg/250px-Hubschrauberflug_Hamburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hubschrauberflug_Hamburg.jpg)

[Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") is Germany's [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population"), with [its seaport](/wiki/Port_of_Hamburg "Port of Hamburg") being the country's largest by volume.

Four sizeable groups of people are referred to as national minorities because their ancestors have lived in their respective regions for centuries:[[233]](#cite_note-BMI_2010-239) There is a [Danish](/wiki/Danes "Danes") minority in the northernmost state of [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein");[[233]](#cite_note-BMI_2010-239) the [Sorbs](/wiki/Sorbs "Sorbs"), a [Slavic population](/wiki/Slavic_people "Slavic people"), are in the [Lusatia](/wiki/Lusatia "Lusatia") region of [Saxony](/wiki/Free_State_of_Saxony "Free State of Saxony") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"); the [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") and [Sinti](/wiki/Sinti "Sinti") live throughout the country; and the [Frisians](/wiki/Frisians "Frisians") are concentrated in Schleswig-Holstein's western coast and in the north-western part of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony").[[233]](#cite_note-BMI_2010-239)

Germany is [a major destination for immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Germany "Immigration to Germany"), ranking second in the world after the United States.[[234]](#cite_note-240) In 2015, following the [2015 refugee crisis](/wiki/2015_European_migrant_crisis "2015 European migrant crisis"), the Population Division of the [United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs](/wiki/United_Nations_Department_of_Economic_and_Social_Affairs "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs") listed Germany as host to the [second-highest number of international migrants worldwide](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_immigrant_population "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by immigrant population"), about 5% or 12 million of all 244 million migrants.[[235]](#cite_note-241) Refugee crises have resulted in substantial population increases;[[236]](#cite_note-242) for example, the major influx of Ukrainian immigrants following the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), whereby over 1.06 million refugees from Ukraine were recorded in Germany as of April 2023.[[237]](#cite_note-243) In 2019, Germany ranked seventh among EU countries in terms of the percentage of migrants in the country's population, at 13.1%.[[238]](#cite_note-244) In 2022, there were 23.8 million people—28.7 percent of the total population—who had a [migration background](/wiki/Migration_background "Migration background").[[239]](#cite_note-245)

Germany has [over 2,000 cities and towns](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Germany "List of cities and towns in Germany"), and 11 officially recognised [metropolitan regions](/wiki/Metropolitan_regions_in_Germany "Metropolitan regions in Germany"). The [country's most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population") is [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), and its largest urban area is the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr").[[240]](#cite_note-Demographia-246)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Germany](/wiki/Religion_in_Germany "Religion in Germany")

Further information: [Catholic Church in Germany](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Germany "Catholic Church in Germany"), [Evangelical Church in Germany](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_in_Germany "Evangelical Church in Germany"), [History of the Jews in Germany](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Germany "History of the Jews in Germany"), and [Islam in Germany](/wiki/Islam_in_Germany "Islam in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/K%C3%B6lner_Dom%2C_K%C3%B6ln-Altstadt-Nord_-_Luftaufnahme-0276_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-K%C3%B6lner_Dom%2C_K%C3%B6ln-Altstadt-Nord_-_Luftaufnahme-0276_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6lner_Dom,_K%C3%B6ln-Altstadt-Nord_-_Luftaufnahme-0276_(cropped).jpg)

[Cologne Cathedral](/wiki/Cologne_Cathedral "Cologne Cathedral"), a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

According to the 2022 census, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Germany "Christianity in Germany") is the largest religion in Germany at 49.7% of the population; 23.1% identified as [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") and 25.1% as [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[242]](#cite_note-248)

A study in 2023 estimated that 46.2% of the population are not members of any religious organisation or [denomination](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") and that a majority of the population no longer belongs to a Christian denomination (48.5% Christians).[[243]](#cite_note-249) [Irreligion in Germany](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Germany "Irreligion in Germany") is strongest in major metropolitan areas and throughout the former [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), which used to be predominantly Protestant before the imposition of [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") under communism.[[244]](#cite_note-250)[[245]](#cite_note-georgetown1-251)

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the second-largest religion in the country.[[246]](#cite_note-Zensus_2011-252) In the 2011 census, 1.9% of respondents (1.52 million people) gave their religion as Islam, but this figure is deemed unreliable because a disproportionate number of adherents of this faith (and other religions, such as Judaism) are likely to have made use of their right not to answer the question.[[247]](#cite_note-253) In 2019, there were an estimated 5.3–5.6 million Muslims with a [migrant background](/wiki/Migrant_background "Migrant background")[[g]](#cite_note-255) (6.4–6.7% of the population), in addition to an unknown number of Muslims without a migrant background.[[248]](#cite_note-BAMF2020-254) Most of the Muslims are [Sunnis](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") and [Alevis](/wiki/Alevism "Alevism") from Turkey, but there are a small number of [Shi'ites](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam"), [Ahmadiyyas](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") and other denominations. Other religions each comprise less than one percent of Germany's population.[[246]](#cite_note-Zensus_2011-252)

In 2011, formal members of the [Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Germany "History of the Jews in Germany") represented no more than 0.2% of the total German population, and 60% of them resided in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin").[[249]](#cite_note-256) An estimated 80 to 90 percent of these Jews in Germany are Russian-speaking immigrants from the [former Soviet Union](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states") who came to Germany from the 1980s onwards.[[250]](#cite_note-257)[[251]](#cite_note-258)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Germany](/wiki/Languages_of_Germany "Languages of Germany")

See also: [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Bilinguale_Stra%C3%9Fenbeschilderung_Cottbus_b.jpg/250px-Bilinguale_Stra%C3%9Fenbeschilderung_Cottbus_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bilinguale_Stra%C3%9Fenbeschilderung_Cottbus_b.jpg)

A bilingual street sign in both [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and [Lower Sorbian](/wiki/Lower_Sorbian_language "Lower Sorbian language") in [Cottbus](/wiki/Cottbus "Cottbus") (*Chóśebuz*), [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg")

German is the official and predominantly spoken language in Germany.[[252]](#cite_note-Eurobarometer_Languages-259) It is one of 24 official and working [languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union"), and one of the three [procedural languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union") of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), alongside English and French.[[253]](#cite_note-260) German is the most widely spoken first language in the European Union, with around 100 million native speakers.[[254]](#cite_note-261)

Recognised native minority languages in Germany are [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), [Low Rhenish](/wiki/Low_Rhenish "Low Rhenish"), [Sorbian](/wiki/Sorbian_languages "Sorbian languages"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), [North Frisian](/wiki/North_Frisian_language "North Frisian language") and [Saterland Frisian](/wiki/Saterland_Frisian_language "Saterland Frisian language"); they are officially protected by the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"). The most used immigrant languages are [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language") and other [Balkan languages](/wiki/Balkan_languages "Balkan languages"), as well as [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"). Germans are typically multilingual: 74% of German citizens claim to be able to communicate in at least one foreign language and 35% in at least two.[[255]](#cite_note-EU-Lang-262)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Germany](/wiki/Education_in_Germany "Education in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg)

[Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University"), Germany's oldest institution of higher learning and generally considered one of its most renowned

Responsibility for educational supervision in Germany is primarily organised within the individual [states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany"). Optional [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") education is provided for all children between three and six years old, after which [school attendance is compulsory](/wiki/Schulpflicht "Schulpflicht") for at least nine years depending on the state. Primary education usually lasts for four to six years.[[256]](#cite_note-ED-263) Secondary schooling is divided into tracks based on whether students pursue [academic](/wiki/College-preparatory_school "College-preparatory school") or [vocational education](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education").[[257]](#cite_note-264) A system of apprenticeship called *[Duale Ausbildung](/wiki/Dual_education_system "Dual education system")* leads to a skilled qualification which is almost comparable to an academic degree. It allows students in vocational training to learn in a company as well as in a state-run trade school.[[256]](#cite_note-ED-263) This model is well regarded and reproduced all around the world.[[258]](#cite_note-265)

Most of the [German universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Germany "List of universities in Germany") are public institutions, and students traditionally study without fee payment.[[259]](#cite_note-266) The general requirement for attending university is the *[Abitur](/wiki/Abitur "Abitur")*. According to an OECD report from 2014, Germany is the world's third leading destination for international study.[[260]](#cite_note-267) The established universities in Germany include some of the [oldest in the world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), with [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") (established in 1386), [Leipzig University](/wiki/Leipzig_University "Leipzig University") (established in 1409) and the [University of Rostock](/wiki/University_of_Rostock "University of Rostock") (established in 1419) being the [oldest in the country](/wiki/List_of_oldest_German_universities "List of oldest German universities").[[261]](#cite_note-268) The [Humboldt University of Berlin](/wiki/Humboldt_University_of_Berlin "Humboldt University of Berlin"), founded in 1810 by the liberal educational reformer [Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt"), became the academic [model for many Western universities](/wiki/Humboldtian_model_of_higher_education "Humboldtian model of higher education").[[262]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-270)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Germany](/wiki/Health_in_Germany "Health in Germany")

Further information: [Healthcare in Germany](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Germany "Healthcare in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Heiligen-Geist-Hospital_%28L%C3%BCbeck%29.jpg/250px-Heiligen-Geist-Hospital_%28L%C3%BCbeck%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heiligen-Geist-Hospital_(L%C3%BCbeck).jpg)

The [Hospital of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Hospital_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Hospital of the Holy Spirit") in [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck"), established in 1286, is a precursor to modern hospitals.[[264]](#cite_note-271)

Germany's system of hospitals, called *Krankenhäuser*, dates from medieval times, and the country has the world's oldest [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system, dating from [Bismarck's social legislation](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck#Social_legislation "Otto von Bismarck") of the 1880s.[[265]](#cite_note-272) Since the 1880s, reforms and provisions have ensured a balanced [health care system](/wiki/Health_care_system "Health care system"). The population is covered by a health insurance plan provided by statute, with criteria allowing some groups to opt for a private health insurance contract. According to the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO), Germany's health care system was 77% government-funded and 23% privately funded in 2013.[[266]](#cite_note-health-273) In 2024, Germany spent 12.27% of its GDP on health care.[[267]](#cite_note-274)

Germany [ranked 21st](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") in the world in 2019 in life expectancy with 78.7 years for men and 84.8 years for women according to the WHO, and it had a very low [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") of 4 deaths per 1,000 [live births](/wiki/Live_birth_(human) "Live birth (human)"). In 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit), the principal cause of death was cardiovascular disease, at 37%.[[268]](#cite_note-275) [Obesity in Germany](/wiki/Obesity_in_Germany "Obesity in Germany") has been increasingly cited as a major health issue: a 2014 study showed that 52 percent of the adult German population was overweight or obese.[[269]](#cite_note-276)

Germany has an extensive psychiatric care system, with rising demand for mental health services; roughly 27.8% of adults experience a mental disorder annually.[[270]](#cite_note-277)[[271]](#cite_note-278) While the system offers comprehensive, insurance-covered care, it faces a shortage of psychotherapists and [a pandemic-driven surge](/wiki/Mental_health_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic") in anxiety and depression.[[272]](#cite_note-279) In 2023, 37.7% of adults rated their mental health as excellent or very good.[[273]](#cite_note-280) In 2019, Germany had the third-highest rate of chronic depression among EU countries.[[274]](#cite_note-281)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Germany](/wiki/Culture_of_Germany "Culture of Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Striezelmarkt_2009_00950.jpg/250px-Striezelmarkt_2009_00950.jpg)](/wiki/File:Striezelmarkt_2009_00950.jpg)

The [Striezelmarkt](/wiki/Striezelmarkt "Striezelmarkt"), a [Christmas market](/wiki/Christmas_market "Christmas market") in [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden")

Culture in German states has been shaped by major intellectual and popular currents in Europe, both religious and [secular](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"), and [German scientists](/wiki/List_of_German_scientists_by_century "List of German scientists by century"), writers and philosophers have played a significant role in the development of Western thought.[[275]](#cite_note-282) Global opinion polls from the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") revealed that Germany is recognised for having the most positive influence in the world in 2013[[276]](#cite_note-283) and 2014.[[277]](#cite_note-284)

Germany is well known for such folk festivals as the [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest")[[278]](#cite_note-285) and [Christmas customs](/wiki/Weihnachten "Weihnachten"), which include [Advent wreaths](/wiki/Advent_wreath "Advent wreath"), [Christmas pageants](/wiki/Nativity_play "Nativity play"), [Christmas trees](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree"), [Stollen](/wiki/Stollen "Stollen") cakes, and other practices.[[279]](#cite_note-286) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit), [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") inscribed [55 properties in Germany on the World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_in_Germany "World Heritage Sites in Germany").[[280]](#cite_note-287) There are a number of [public holidays in Germany](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Germany "Public holidays in Germany") determined by each state; 3 October has been a [national day](/wiki/National_day "National day") of Germany since 1990, celebrated as the *Tag der Deutschen Einheit* ([German Unity Day](/wiki/German_Unity_Day "German Unity Day")).[[281]](#cite_note-288)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Germany](/wiki/Music_of_Germany "Music of Germany")

See also: [Opera in German](/wiki/Opera_in_German "Opera in German")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Joseph_Karl_Stieler%27s_Beethoven_mit_dem_Manuskript_der_Missa_solemnis.jpg/250px-Joseph_Karl_Stieler%27s_Beethoven_mit_dem_Manuskript_der_Missa_solemnis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Karl_Stieler%27s_Beethoven_mit_dem_Manuskript_der_Missa_solemnis.jpg)

[Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven") (1770–1827), one of the most famed composers of [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), was born in [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn").

German [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music_era "Classical music era") includes works by some of the world's most well-known composers. [Dieterich Buxtehude](/wiki/Dieterich_Buxtehude "Dieterich Buxtehude"), [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") and [Georg Friedrich Händel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel") were influential composers of the [Baroque period](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music"). [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven") was a crucial figure in the transition between the Classical and [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") eras. [Carl Maria von Weber](/wiki/Carl_Maria_von_Weber "Carl Maria von Weber"), [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn"), [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann") and [Johannes Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms") were significant Romantic composers. [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner") was known for his operas. [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss") was a leading composer of the late Romantic and early [modern](/wiki/20th-century_classical_music "20th-century classical music") eras. [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen") and [Wolfgang Rihm](/wiki/Wolfgang_Rihm "Wolfgang Rihm") are important composers of the 20th and early 21st centuries.[[282]](#cite_note-289)

In 2013, Germany was the second-largest music market in Europe, and [fourth-largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_recorded_music_markets "List of largest recorded music markets").[[283]](#cite_note-290) German popular music of the 20th and 21st centuries includes the movements of [Neue Deutsche Welle](/wiki/Neue_Deutsche_Welle "Neue Deutsche Welle"), [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"), [Ostrock](/wiki/Ostrock "Ostrock"), [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music")/[rock](/wiki/German_rock "German rock"), [punk](/wiki/German_punk "German punk"), [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock"), [indie](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"), [Volksmusik](/wiki/Volksmusik "Volksmusik") (folk music), [schlager pop](/wiki/Schlager_music "Schlager music") and [German hip hop](/wiki/German_hip_hop "German hip hop"). German [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") gained global influence, with [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk") and [Tangerine Dream](/wiki/Tangerine_Dream "Tangerine Dream") pioneering in this genre.[[284]](#cite_note-291) DJs and artists of the [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") and [house music](/wiki/House_music "House music") scenes of Germany have become well known (e.g. [Paul van Dyk](/wiki/Paul_van_Dyk "Paul van Dyk"), [Felix Jaehn](/wiki/Felix_Jaehn "Felix Jaehn"), [Paul Kalkbrenner](/wiki/Paul_Kalkbrenner "Paul Kalkbrenner"), [Robin Schulz](/wiki/Robin_Schulz "Robin Schulz") and [Scooter](/wiki/Scooter_(band) "Scooter (band)")).[[285]](#cite_note-292)

### Art, design and architecture

Main articles: [German art](/wiki/German_art "German art"), [Architecture of Germany](/wiki/Architecture_of_Germany "Architecture of Germany"), and [German fashion](/wiki/German_fashion "German fashion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg/250px-Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg)

[C.D. Friedrich](/wiki/Caspar_David_Friedrich "Caspar David Friedrich"), *[Wanderer above the Sea of Fog](/wiki/Wanderer_above_the_Sea_of_Fog "Wanderer above the Sea of Fog")* (1818)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Franz_Marc_020.jpg/250px-Franz_Marc_020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Franz_Marc_020.jpg)

[Franz Marc](/wiki/Franz_Marc "Franz Marc"), *Roe Deer in the Forest* (1914)

[German painters](/wiki/German_painters "German painters") have influenced [Western art](/wiki/Art_of_Europe "Art of Europe"). [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer"), [Hans Holbein the Younger](/wiki/Hans_Holbein_the_Younger "Hans Holbein the Younger"), [Matthias Grünewald](/wiki/Matthias_Gr%C3%BCnewald "Matthias Grünewald") and [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder") were important German artists of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), [Johann Baptist Zimmermann](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Zimmermann "Johann Baptist Zimmermann") of the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque"), [Caspar David Friedrich](/wiki/Caspar_David_Friedrich "Caspar David Friedrich") and [Carl Spitzweg](/wiki/Carl_Spitzweg "Carl Spitzweg") of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), [Max Liebermann](/wiki/Max_Liebermann "Max Liebermann") of [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") and [Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst") of [Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"). Several German art groups formed in the 20th century; *[Die Brücke](/wiki/Die_Br%C3%BCcke "Die Brücke")* (The Bridge) and *[Der Blaue Reiter](/wiki/Der_Blaue_Reiter "Der Blaue Reiter")* (The Blue Rider) influenced the development of [expressionism](/wiki/German_Expressionism "German Expressionism") in Munich and Berlin. The [New Objectivity](/wiki/New_Objectivity "New Objectivity") arose in response to expressionism during the Weimar Republic. After World War II, broad trends in German art include [neo-expressionism](/wiki/Neo-expressionism "Neo-expressionism") and the [New Leipzig School](/wiki/New_Leipzig_School "New Leipzig School").[[286]](#cite_note-groveart-293)

German designers became early leaders of modern [product design](/wiki/Product_design "Product design").[[287]](#cite_note-294) The [Berlin Fashion Week](/wiki/Berlin_Fashion_Week "Berlin Fashion Week") and the fashion trade fair [Bread & Butter](/wiki/Bread_%26_Butter_(tradeshow) "Bread & Butter (tradeshow)") are held twice a year.[[288]](#cite_note-295)

Architectural contributions from Germany include the [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian_architecture "Carolingian architecture") and [Ottonian](/wiki/Ottonian_architecture "Ottonian architecture") styles, which were precursors of [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"). [Brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic") is a distinctive medieval style that evolved in Germany. Also in [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") and [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") art, regional and typically German elements evolved (e.g. [Weser Renaissance](/wiki/Weser_Renaissance "Weser Renaissance")).[[286]](#cite_note-groveart-293) [Vernacular architecture](/wiki/Vernacular_architecture "Vernacular architecture") in Germany is often identified by [its timber framing](/wiki/Fachwerkhaus "Fachwerkhaus") (*Fachwerk*) traditions and varies across regions, and among carpentry styles.[[289]](#cite_note-Heinrich_Stiewe_2007-296) When industrialisation spread across Europe, [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism") and a distinctive style of [historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)") developed in Germany, sometimes referred to as *[Gründerzeit](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnderzeit "Gründerzeit")* *style*. [Expressionist architecture](/wiki/Expressionist_architecture "Expressionist architecture") developed in the 1910s in Germany and influenced [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") and other modern styles. Germany was particularly important in the early [modernist movement](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture"): it is the home of [Werkbund](/wiki/Deutscher_Werkbund "Deutscher Werkbund") initiated by [Hermann Muthesius](/wiki/Hermann_Muthesius "Hermann Muthesius") ([New Objectivity](/wiki/New_Objectivity_(architecture) "New Objectivity (architecture)")), and of the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") movement founded by [Walter Gropius](/wiki/Walter_Gropius "Walter Gropius").[[286]](#cite_note-groveart-293) [Ludwig Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Ludwig_Mies_van_der_Rohe "Ludwig Mies van der Rohe") became one of the world's most renowned architects in the second half of the 20th century; he conceived of the glass façade [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper").[[290]](#cite_note-297) Renowned contemporary [German architects](/wiki/List_of_German_architects "List of German architects") and offices include [Pritzker Prize](/wiki/Pritzker_Prize "Pritzker Prize") winners [Gottfried Böhm](/wiki/Gottfried_B%C3%B6hm "Gottfried Böhm") and [Frei Otto](/wiki/Frei_Otto "Frei Otto").[[291]](#cite_note-298)

### Literature and philosophy

Main articles: [German literature](/wiki/German_literature "German literature") and [German philosophy](/wiki/German_philosophy "German philosophy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Grimm.jpg/250px-Grimm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grimm.jpg)

[Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm"), who collected popular German folk tales and published them in [a collection](/wiki/Grimms%27_Fairy_Tales "Grimms' Fairy Tales")

German literature can be traced back to the Middle Ages and the works of writers such as [Walther von der Vogelweide](/wiki/Walther_von_der_Vogelweide "Walther von der Vogelweide") and [Wolfram von Eschenbach](/wiki/Wolfram_von_Eschenbach "Wolfram von Eschenbach"). Well-known German authors include [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"), [Friedrich Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller"), [Gotthold Ephraim Lessing](/wiki/Gotthold_Ephraim_Lessing "Gotthold Ephraim Lessing") and [Theodor Fontane](/wiki/Theodor_Fontane "Theodor Fontane"). The collections of folk tales published by the [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm") popularised [German folklore](/wiki/German_folklore "German folklore") on an international level.[[292]](#cite_note-D99ff-299) The Grimms also gathered and codified regional variants of the German language, grounding their work in historical principles; their *[Deutsches Wörterbuch](/wiki/Deutsches_W%C3%B6rterbuch "Deutsches Wörterbuch")*, or German Dictionary, sometimes called the Grimm dictionary, was begun in 1838 and the first volumes published in 1854.[[293]](#cite_note-DWBhistory-300)

Influential authors of the 20th century include [Gerhart Hauptmann](/wiki/Gerhart_Hauptmann "Gerhart Hauptmann"), [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), [Hermann Hesse](/wiki/Hermann_Hesse "Hermann Hesse"), [Heinrich Böll](/wiki/Heinrich_B%C3%B6ll "Heinrich Böll"), and [Günter Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "Günter Grass").[[294]](#cite_note-301) The German book market is the third-largest in the world, after the United States and China.[[295]](#cite_note-302) The [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") is the most important in the world for international deals and trading, with a tradition spanning over 500 years.[[296]](#cite_note-303) The [Leipzig Book Fair](/wiki/Leipzig_Book_Fair "Leipzig Book Fair") also retains a major position in Europe.[[297]](#cite_note-304)

German philosophy is historically significant: [Gottfried Leibniz](/wiki/Gottfried_Leibniz "Gottfried Leibniz")'s contributions to [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"); the [enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") philosophy by [Immanuel Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant"); the establishment of classical [German idealism](/wiki/German_idealism "German idealism") by [Johann Gottlieb Fichte](/wiki/Johann_Gottlieb_Fichte "Johann Gottlieb Fichte"), [Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel") and [Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Joseph_Schelling "Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling"); [Arthur Schopenhauer](/wiki/Arthur_Schopenhauer "Arthur Schopenhauer")'s composition of metaphysical pessimism; the formulation of [communist theory](/wiki/Communist_theory "Communist theory") by [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"); [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche")'s development of [perspectivism](/wiki/Perspectivism "Perspectivism"); [Gottlob Frege](/wiki/Gottlob_Frege "Gottlob Frege")'s contributions to the dawn of [analytic philosophy](/wiki/Analytic_philosophy "Analytic philosophy"); [Martin Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger")'s works on Being; [Oswald Spengler](/wiki/Oswald_Spengler "Oswald Spengler")'s historical philosophy; and the development of the [Frankfurt School](/wiki/Frankfurt_School "Frankfurt School") have all been very influential.[[298]](#cite_note-305)

### Media

Main article: [Media of Germany](/wiki/Media_of_Germany "Media of Germany")

Further information: [Cinema of Germany](/wiki/Cinema_of_Germany "Cinema of Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Filmstudio_Babelsberg_Eingang.jpg/250px-Filmstudio_Babelsberg_Eingang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filmstudio_Babelsberg_Eingang.jpg)

[Babelsberg Studio](/wiki/Babelsberg_Studio "Babelsberg Studio") in [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam"), the first large-scale film studio in the world

The largest internationally operating [media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media") companies in Germany are [Bertelsmann](/wiki/Bertelsmann "Bertelsmann"), [Axel Springer SE](/wiki/Axel_Springer_AG "Axel Springer AG") and [ProSiebenSat.1 Media](/wiki/ProSiebenSat.1_Media "ProSiebenSat.1 Media"). [Germany's television market](/wiki/Television_in_Germany "Television in Germany") is the largest in Europe, with over 38 million TV households as of 2012.[[299]](#cite_note-306) Around 90% of German households have cable or satellite TV, with a variety of [free-to-view public](/wiki/Public_broadcasting#Germany "Public broadcasting") and [commercial](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") channels.[[300]](#cite_note-media-307) There are more than [300 public and private radio stations in Germany](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Germany "List of radio stations in Germany"); Germany's national radio network is the [Deutschlandradio](/wiki/Deutschlandradio "Deutschlandradio"), while the public [Deutsche Welle](/wiki/Deutsche_Welle "Deutsche Welle") is the main radio and television broadcaster in foreign languages.[[300]](#cite_note-media-307) Germany's print media market is the largest in Europe.[[300]](#cite_note-media-307) The [German newspapers](/wiki/German_newspapers "German newspapers") with the highest circulation are *[Bild](/wiki/Bild "Bild")*, *[Süddeutsche Zeitung](/wiki/S%C3%BCddeutsche_Zeitung "Süddeutsche Zeitung")*, *[Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung](/wiki/Frankfurter_Allgemeine_Zeitung "Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung")* and *[Die Welt](/wiki/Die_Welt "Die Welt")*.[[300]](#cite_note-media-307) The largest [German magazines](/wiki/German_magazines "German magazines") include *[ADAC Motorwelt](/wiki/ADAC_Motorwelt "ADAC Motorwelt")* and *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")*.[[300]](#cite_note-media-307) Germany has [a large video gaming market](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Germany "Video gaming in Germany"), with over 34 million players nationwide.[[301]](#cite_note-308) The annual [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") held in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") is the world's largest [gaming convention](/wiki/Gaming_convention "Gaming convention").[[302]](#cite_note-309)

German cinema has made major technical and artistic contributions to film. The first works of the [Skladanowsky Brothers](/wiki/Max_Skladanowsky "Max Skladanowsky") were shown to an audience in 1895. The renowned [Babelsberg Studio](/wiki/Babelsberg_Studio "Babelsberg Studio") in [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam") was established in 1912, thus being the first large-scale film studio in the world. Early German cinema was particularly influential with [German expressionists](/wiki/German_expressionist_cinema "German expressionist cinema") such as [Robert Wiene](/wiki/Robert_Wiene "Robert Wiene") and [Friedrich Wilhelm Murnau](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Murnau "Friedrich Wilhelm Murnau"). Director [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang")'s *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* (1927) is referred to as the first major science-fiction film. After 1945, many of the films of the immediate post-war period can be characterised as *[Trümmerfilm](/wiki/Rubble_film "Rubble film")* (rubble film). East German film was dominated by the state-owned film studio [DEFA](/wiki/DEFA_(film_studio) "DEFA (film studio)"), while the dominant genre in West Germany was the *[Heimatfilm](/wiki/Heimatfilm "Heimatfilm")* ("homeland film").[[303]](#cite_note-310) The [Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film") ("Oscar") went to the German production *[The Tin Drum](/wiki/The_Tin_Drum_(film) "The Tin Drum (film)")* (*Die Blechtrommel*) in 1979, to *[Nowhere in Africa](/wiki/Nowhere_in_Africa "Nowhere in Africa")* (*Nirgendwo in Afrika*) in 2002, and to *[The Lives of Others](/wiki/The_Lives_of_Others "The Lives of Others")* (*Das Leben der Anderen*) in 2007. [Various Germans](/wiki/List_of_German-speaking_Academy_Award_winners_and_nominees "List of German-speaking Academy Award winners and nominees") won an Oscar for their performances in films. The annual [European Film Awards](/wiki/European_Film_Award "European Film Award") ceremony is held every other year in Berlin, home of the [European Film Academy](/wiki/European_Film_Academy "European Film Academy"). The [Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival") known as "Berlinale", awarding the "[Golden Bear](/wiki/Golden_Bear "Golden Bear")" and held annually since 1951, is one of the world's leading [film festivals](/wiki/Film_festival "Film festival"). The "Lolas" are annually awarded in Berlin, at the [German Film Awards](/wiki/Deutscher_Filmpreis "Deutscher Filmpreis").[[304]](#cite_note-311)

### Cuisine

Main article: [German cuisine](/wiki/German_cuisine "German cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/BavarianLunch.jpg/250px-BavarianLunch.jpg)](/wiki/File:BavarianLunch.jpg)

Bavarian [Bratwurst](/wiki/Bratwurst "Bratwurst") with mustard, a [pretzel](/wiki/Pretzel "Pretzel"), and [German beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Germany "Beer in Germany")

[Bread](/wiki/German_cuisine#Bread "German cuisine") is a significant part of German cuisine, with German bakeries producing about 600 main types of bread and 1,200 types of pastries and [rolls](/wiki/Bread_roll "Bread roll") (*Brötchen*).[[305]](#cite_note-312) [German cheeses](/wiki/List_of_German_cheeses "List of German cheeses") account for about 22% of all cheese produced in Europe.[[306]](#cite_note-313) Germans produce their ubiquitous sausages in almost 1,500 varieties, including [Bratwurst](/wiki/Bratwurst "Bratwurst") and [Weißwurst](/wiki/Wei%C3%9Fwurst "Weißwurst").[[307]](#cite_note-314)

The national alcoholic drink is [beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Germany "Beer in Germany").[[308]](#cite_note-315) Germany's beer consumption per capita stood at 110 litres (24 imp gal; 29 US gal) in 2013 and remains among the [highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_beer_consumption_per_capita "List of countries by beer consumption per capita").[[309]](#cite_note-316) [German beer purity regulations](/wiki/Reinheitsgebot "Reinheitsgebot") date back to the 16th century.[[310]](#cite_note-317) [Wine](/wiki/German_wine "German wine") has become popular in many parts of the country, especially near the [German wine regions](/wiki/List_of_German_wine_regions "List of German wine regions").[[311]](#cite_note-318) In 2019, Germany was the [ninth-largest wine producer in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wine_production "List of countries by wine production").[[312]](#cite_note-319)

The 2018 *[Michelin Guide](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide")* awarded [eleven restaurants in Germany three stars](/wiki/List_of_Michelin_starred_restaurants#Germany "List of Michelin starred restaurants"), giving the country a cumulative total of [300 stars](/wiki/List_of_Michelin-starred_restaurants_in_Germany "List of Michelin-starred restaurants in Germany").[[313]](#cite_note-320)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Germany](/wiki/Sport_in_Germany "Sport in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Germany_champions_2014_FIFA_World_Cup.jpg/250px-Germany_champions_2014_FIFA_World_Cup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germany_champions_2014_FIFA_World_Cup.jpg)

The [German national football team](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") after winning the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") for the fourth time in 2014

[Football](/wiki/Football_in_Germany "Football in Germany") is the most popular sport in Germany. With more than 7 million official members, the [German Football Association](/wiki/German_Football_Association "German Football Association") (*Deutscher Fußball-Bund*) is the largest single-sport organisation worldwide,[[314]](#cite_note-321) and the German top league, the [Bundesliga](/wiki/Bundesliga "Bundesliga"), attracts the second-highest [average attendance](/wiki/List_of_attendance_figures_at_domestic_professional_sports_leagues "List of attendance figures at domestic professional sports leagues") of all professional sports leagues in the world.[[315]](#cite_note-322) The [German men's national football team](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") won the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [1954](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup "1954 FIFA World Cup"), [1974](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup"), [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup"), and [2014](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"),[[316]](#cite_note-323) the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") in [1972](/wiki/1972_UEFA_European_Championship "1972 UEFA European Championship"), [1980](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1980 "UEFA Euro 1980") and [1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"),[[317]](#cite_note-324) and the [FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Confederations_Cup "FIFA Confederations Cup") in [2017](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup").[[318]](#cite_note-325)

Germany is one of the world's leading [motor sports](/wiki/Motorsport_in_Germany "Motorsport in Germany") countries, with constructors such as [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW") and [Mercedes](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz") playing prominent roles in international competition. [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") has won the [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans") race 19 times, and [Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi") 13 times, as of April 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Germany&action=edit).[[319]](#cite_note-326) The driver [Michael Schumacher](/wiki/Michael_Schumacher "Michael Schumacher") has set many motor sport records, having won seven [Formula One World Drivers' Championships](/wiki/List_of_Formula_One_World_Drivers%27_Champions "List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions").[[320]](#cite_note-327) [Sebastian Vettel](/wiki/Sebastian_Vettel "Sebastian Vettel") is also among the most successful [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") drivers of all time.[[321]](#cite_note-328)

[German athletes](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") historically have been successful contenders in the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), ranking third in an [all-time Olympic Games medal count](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_count "All-time Olympic Games medal count") when combining [East](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") and [West German](/wiki/West_German "West German") medals prior to [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification").[[322]](#cite_note-329) In 1936, Berlin hosted the [Summer Games](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics") and the [Winter Games](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics") in [Garmisch-Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen").[[323]](#cite_note-330) [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") hosted the [Summer Games of 1972](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics").[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarge2007337-331)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** From 1952 to 1990, the entire "Das Lied der Deutschen" was the national anthem, but only the third verse was sung on official occasions. Since 1991, the third verse alone has been the national anthem.[[1]](#cite_note-PresidentsOffice-1)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Berlin is the sole constitutional capital and *de jure* seat of government, but the former provisional capital of the Federal Republic of Germany, [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), has the special title of "federal city" (*Bundesstadt*) and is the primary seat of six ministries.[[2]](#cite_note-3)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), [Sorbian](/wiki/Sorbian_languages "Sorbian languages"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), and [Frisian](/wiki/Frisian_languages "Frisian languages") are recognised by the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[3]](#cite_note-5)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Deutschland*, German: [[ˈdɔʏtʃlant]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Deutschland.ogg "File:De-Deutschland.ogg")
5. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bundesrepublik Deutschland*, German: [[ˈbʊndəsʁepuˌbliːk ˈdɔʏtʃlant]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Bundesrepublik_Deutschland.ogg "File:De-Bundesrepublik Deutschland.ogg")[[10]](#cite_note-14)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-turkey_238-0)** Excluding [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), which only has around 3% of its territory and 10% of its population in Europe[[232]](#cite_note-237)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-255)** A migrant background is defined as having been born or having at least one parent born in a country from a prespecified list of countries with a significant Muslim population, or as having citizenship or at least one parent with citizenship of one of these countries.[[248]](#cite_note-BAMF2020-254)
